claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 10 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 10 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To get started, I need to ask you a few questions about your situation.

**First, let me ask about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is your child? 

The age category is important because:
- **Under 16**: Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- **16-19**: Young people aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- **20 or over**: Generally not eligible for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you're asking about more than one child, let me know about each of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for your help. I'm asking about my son Alex - he's 10 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! So you're asking about your son **Alex** who is **10 years old**.

Since Alex is under 16, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news - since Alex is 10 years old, he's in the under-16 category. Now I need to check a few more conditions:

**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?**

For a 10-year-old, this would typically be "yes" (not in paid work), but I need to confirm this with you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all - he's only 10!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Of course! That makes perfect sense. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Is Alex NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right** (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

In other words, does Alex receive any benefits like ESA or Universal Credit in his own name? For most 10-year-olds, this would typically be "no" - they don't receive these benefits.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't get any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next:

**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship?**

This question checks whether he's in a disqualifying apprenticeship program. For a 10-year-old, this would typically be "no" as apprenticeships are generally for young people aged 16 and over.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in an apprenticeship - he's still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status:

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**, OR
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, OR
3. You have pre-settled status and meet certain conditions (working, studying, or have sufficient resources), OR
4. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

For most people, the first option is the most common - do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Leeds.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check if you're responsible for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies:

1. **You live with Alex**, OR
2. You contribute to his upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit - or equivalent in kind - towards his care, such as food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents), OR
3. You're paying maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you? For example, do you live with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't live with me. I do help out with some money for clothes and things like that, but it's not very much - maybe about twenty quid a week.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me check the current Child Benefit rates to see if your contribution meets the requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I can see that the exact Child Benefit rates aren't specified in my reference documents, but let me explain the requirement:

To be considered "responsible" through contributions, you need to pay **at least the same amount as Child Benefit** (or equivalent in kind) towards Alex's care. 

Currently (as of 2024), Child Benefit is approximately:
- **£25.60 per week** for the eldest or only child
- **£16.95 per week** for additional children

You mentioned you contribute about **£20 per week** for clothes and things. This is less than the full Child Benefit rate for an only/eldest child.

**However, I have a few questions to clarify:**

1. Is Alex your eldest or only child, or do you have other children?
2. When you say £20 per week, does this include everything you provide (food, clothes, pocket money, housing contributions, presents), or are there other contributions you make?
3. Do you have any formal maintenance agreement or court order in place?

This will help me determine if your contributions meet the threshold for being considered "responsible" for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is my only child. The £20 a week is what I can manage at the moment - it's for clothes and bits and pieces for him. I don't have a formal maintenance agreement or anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Based on what you've told me:

- You don't live with Alex
- You contribute about £20 per week (which is below the Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week for an only child)
- You don't have a formal maintenance agreement or court order

Unfortunately, this means you don't meet the "responsibility" requirement for Child Benefit. The rules require that you either:
1. Live with the child, OR
2. Contribute at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their upkeep, OR
3. Pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Since your contribution is below the Child Benefit threshold and you don't live with Alex, you wouldn't meet this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Decision

I'm sorry to inform you that **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** based on your current circumstances.

### Why you're not eligible:

While Alex qualifies as a child who could be covered by Child Benefit (he's 10 years old, not working, not receiving benefits, and not in an apprenticeship), and you meet the UK residency requirements, **you don't meet the "responsibility" requirement**.

To be considered responsible for a child, you must:
- **Live with the child**, OR
- **Contribute at least the Child Benefit amount** (approximately £25.60/week for an only child) towards their upkeep, OR
- **Pay maintenance under a court order or agreement**

Your current contribution of about £20 per week falls below the required threshold.

### What you could consider:

1. **If you can increase your contributions** to at least the Child Benefit amount (£25.60/week), you may become eligible
2. **The person Alex lives with** (presumably his other parent or guardian) may be eligible to claim Child Benefit if they meet the responsibility requirement
3. If you're interested in establishing a formal maintenance arrangement, you could explore options through the Child Maintenance Service

I understand this may be disappointing news. The good news is that someone who lives with Alex and cares for him day-to-day should be able to claim Child Benefit for him.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or your situation?

reasonings: Alex is 10 years old and meets the basic age requirement (under 16). He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Leeds). However, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement because: (1) the claimant does not live with Alex, (2) the claimant contributes approximately £20 per week which is below the Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week for an only/eldest child, and (3) there is no formal maintenance agreement or court order in place. Since none of the three responsibility pathways are satisfied, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is asking about one child (Alex, age 10). While Alex qualifies as a qualifying child and the claimant meets residency requirements, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement as they do not live with Alex and their financial contributions fall below the required threshold.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.guidance: You must be responsible for the child, which means either living with them OR paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, housing, pocket money, etc.). This can include maintenance payments under court order.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 10 years old and meets the basic age requirement (under 16). He is not working, not receiving benefits in his own right, and not in an apprenticeship. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (lives in Leeds). However, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement because: (1) the claimant does not live with Alex, (2) the claimant contributes approximately £20 per week which is below the Child Benefit rate of approximately £25.60 per week for an only/eldest child, and (3) there is no formal maintenance agreement or court order in place. Since none of the three responsibility pathways are satisfied, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is asking about one child (Alex, age 10). While Alex qualifies as a qualifying child and the claimant meets residency requirements, the claimant does not meet the responsibility requirement as they do not live with Alex and their financial contributions fall below the required threshold.
